title: Nginx publishes unauthenticated CMS Smartling API
source: Any client/browser origin that can reach /cms-smartling/api/
sink_or_broken_control: nginx rewrites to backend without auth or allowlist; backend allows all origins and has unauthenticated state-changing routes
impact: Create Smartling jobs, import/sync/stage translations, mock publish translations, and read request/translation workflow data
controls_counterevidence: TLS is present and backend binds localhost, but nginx deliberately publishes the API and no auth_basic/allow/deny/app auth was found
validation_facts: newrequestform.conf:135-143 proxies /cms-smartling/api/ to 127.0.0.1:17817; backend/server.mjs:179-185 and 1300-1308 set wildcard CORS; backend/server.mjs:1342-1415 dispatches state-changing routes without auth
attack_path_facts: POST /cms-smartling/api/custom-translation-requests or /cms-smartling/api/translations/stage reaches backend and mutates Smartling/local translation state

title: Release metadata can replace download hrefs with unvalidated URLs
source: release-info.json if deployed metadata is tampered independently of index.html
sink_or_broken_control: releaseInfo.downloads.chromium/firefox assigned directly to anchor href without scheme/path validation
impact: Poisoned metadata can point users to attacker-controlled extension ZIPs or script URLs
controls_counterevidence: Visible text is escaped and the normal generator emits relative downloads/*.zip paths; no runtime validation protects against metadata-only tamper
validation_facts: docs/index.html:100-107 fetches release-info.json; docs/index.html:134-157 trusts downloads fields and assigns hrefs; nginx serves release-info.json and downloads at scripts/newrequestform.conf:113-123
attack_path_facts: Attacker changes release-info.json downloads.chromium to https://attacker.example/package.zip; user clicks landing-page download and loads malicious extension ZIP
